summary: Skepticism is a broad philosophical position that doubts the certainty of knowledge. The clues distinguish between Pyrrhonian skepticism (founded by Pyrrho, systematized by Sextus Empiricus) and Academic skepticism (founded by Arcesilaus at Plato's Academy). Key concepts include epoche (suspension of judgment), ataraxia (freedom from disturbance), and the Problem of the Criterion.

works.name: Pyrrhonian Skepticism
works.description: The branch of ancient skepticism founded by Pyrrho and systematized by Sextus Empiricus in his Outlines of Pyrrhonism. Pyrrhonian skeptics advocated epoche (suspension of judgment) as the path to ataraxia (freedom from disturbance). This tradition heavily influenced Michel de Montaigne, who had quotes from it inscribed on the rafters of his tower study in Dordogne. Montaigne's personal medal bore the motto 'what do I know?' on one side and the Greek word 'epoche' on the other.

clues.clue: Sextus Empiricus's Outlines of Pyrrhonism is the key text of this tradition
examples: Montaigne's version of this philosophy was particularly inspired by Sextus Empiricus's Outlines of Pyrrhonism

clues.clue: Epoche (suspension of judgment) is central to ancient skepticism; Husserl later used this term for phenomenological 'bracketing'
examples: Montaigne's personal medal had the motto 'what do I know?' on one side and this Greek word central to ancient skepticism on the other

clues.clue: Ataraxia (freedom from disturbance) was the goal achieved through epoche
examples: Ancient skeptics espoused epoche, or the suspension of judgment, as the best way to achieve this Greek-language mental state usually translated as 'freedom from disturbance'

clues.clue: Pyrrho is the founder of this branch of skepticism
examples: this ancient school of thought, one branch of which was founded by Pyrrho

works.name: Academic Skepticism
works.description: The branch of skepticism that developed within Plato's Academy, inaugurated by Arcesilaus (the sixth scholarch) during the 'Middle' Academy phase and continued by Carneades in the 'New Academy.' Academic skeptics advocated for akatalepsia (the idea that nothing can be known). Carneades introduced probabilistic reasoning as a way to still achieve knowledge despite the absence of cognitive impressions.

clues.clue: Arcesilaus founded Academic skepticism and was the sixth scholarch of the Academy
examples: Arcesilaus promulgated the 'Academic' form of this philosophical view, which advocates for akatalepsia

clues.clue: Carneades was a leading Academic skeptic of the New Academy who emphasized probabilism
examples: this rival of Chrysippus names a 'plank' in a thought experiment used to discuss self-defense killings

clues.clue: The Academy ended during the First Mithridatic War when Philo of Larissa fled to Rome
examples: This institution ended during the First Mithridatic War when Philo of Larissa fled to Rome

works.name: Influence on Later Philosophy
works.description: Skepticism had a significant influence on later thinkers, particularly David Hume and Michel de Montaigne. Hume extended skepticism to encompass causation, arguing that 'constant conjunction' leads us to unjustly posit cause-and-effect out of habit. Montaigne's essay 'Apology for Raymond Sebond' drew heavily from Pyrrhonian skepticism, and Roderick Chisholm traced the 'Problem of the Criterion' through Montaigne's version of a skeptical argument named for a circle (diallelus).

clues.clue: Hume extended skepticism to causation, arguing constant conjunction leads to unjust assumption of cause-and-effect out of habit
examples: Hume extends his skepticism to encompass this phenomenon by claiming that when we observe 'constant conjunction,' we unjustly posit it out of habit

clues.clue: Montaigne's 'Apology for Raymond Sebond' was influenced by skepticism, particularly Sextus Empiricus
examples: this tradition that influenced Michel de Montaigne's essay 'Apology for Raymond Sebond'

clues.clue: Roderick Chisholm traced the Problem of the Criterion through Montaigne's version of a circular skeptical argument (diallelus)
examples: Roderick Chisholm traced the 'Problem of the Criterion' of knowledge through Montaigne's version of a skeptical argument named for this shape

clues.clue: Alvin Plantinga's 1967 book God and Other Minds analogizes the problem of other minds to believing in God
examples: A 1967 book by Alvin Plantinga analogizes an epistemological problem named for these things to believing in God

comprehensive_summary:
Skepticism is a broad philosophical tradition that questions the certainty of knowledge. The clues reveal two main branches: Pyrrhonian skepticism, founded by Pyrrho and systematized by Sextus Empiricus in his Outlines of Pyrrhonism, and Academic skepticism, which developed within Plato's Academy under Arcesilaus and later Carneades.

Pyrrhonian skeptics advocate epoche, or the suspension of judgment, as the best path to ataraxia (freedom from disturbance). This Greek term epoche was later adopted by Edmund Husserl for phenomenological 'bracketing.' The tradition heavily influenced Michel de Montaigne, who had skeptical quotes inscribed on the rafters of his tower study in Dordogne and wore a personal medal with the motto 'what do I know?' on one side and 'epoche' on the other. Montaigne's essay 'Apology for Raymond Sebond' was particularly inspired by Sextus Empiricus.

Academic skepticism began with Arcesilaus, the sixth scholarch of the Academy, who inaugurated its 'Middle' phase and promoted akatalepsia (the idea that nothing can be known). Carneades continued this tradition in the 'New Academy,' introducing probabilistic reasoning. The Academic tradition ended during the First Mithridatic War when Philo of Larissa fled to Rome.

David Hume is a major figure in the skeptical tradition. In his Treatise of Human Nature, he noted that the coherence of an object leads 'vulgar' people to reject skepticism. In An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding, he extended skepticism to causation, arguing that constant conjunction leads us to unjustly posit cause-and-effect out of habit. Hume's skeptical discussion of sympathy assumes the existence of other minds, a problem later addressed by Alvin Plantinga in his 1967 book God and Other Minds. Roderick Chisholm traced the Problem of the Criterion through Montaigne's circular skeptical argument (diallelus).
